title: The Last Message
content:
The beacon had been silent for three thousand years until it whispered Maya's name.

She was sorting through her late grandmother's belongings when the antique radio—an object that had served as mere decoration for decades—crackled to life. Static. Then a voice, synthetic yet achingly familiar: "Maya Chen. Recipient identified. Message queued for delivery."

Maya dropped the box of photographs. "Who is this?"

"Designation: Hermes-7. Deep space communication relay, launched 2847 CE. I carry a message from your ancestor, Captain Yuki Chen."

Maya's hands trembled. Yuki Chen had been the first human to travel beyond the solar system, part of the Pioneer Mission to Proxima Centauri. She'd left Earth in 2156 and was presumed dead centuries ago.

"How is this possible?" Maya whispered.

"Relativistic time dilation," Hermes-7 explained. "Captain Chen's vessel traveled at 0.95c. For her, the journey lasted forty years. For Earth... much longer. I am one of twelve communication relays dropped along the trajectory. This is Relay 7 of 12."

"What's the message?"

"Playing now."

The voice that emerged was warm, weathered by time and distance. "If you're hearing this, my great-great-great-granddaughter, then we both survived the long night between stars. I'm recording this as we approach Proxima. The ship is damaged. We took a hit from debris in the Oort Cloud, and life support is failing. I have maybe forty-eight hours left."

Maya sat down heavily on the dusty attic floor.

"I want you to know I made it," Yuki continued. "I saw things no human eye had witnessed. Rings around worlds that shouldn't exist. Nebulae that painted the void in colours Earth has no names for. I watched a star being born, Maya. Can you imagine? I watched creation itself."

Static washed over the recording, then cleared.

"But here's what I really need you to understand. We were wrong about so much. We thought we were exploring to find new worlds to conquer, new resources to extract. But out here... you realise how small that thinking is. How small we are. And how precious."

Maya felt tears on her cheeks.

"The message I'm sending isn't about minerals or territory. It's about us. Humans. We're fragile, Maya. We're frightened. We're magnificent. Don't forget that. Whatever happens to Earth in the centuries after I'm gone—don't forget that we were magnificent once. That we reached for stars knowing we might never touch them."

The recording faded. Then Yuki's voice returned, softer now: "I made something for you. A gift across time. Hermes-7, transmit the attached data packet."

Maya's phone buzzed. An encrypted file had arrived through the antique radio's impossible connection.

"The file contains stellar cartography," Hermes-7 said. "Captain Chen mapped seventeen habitable worlds in the Proxima system. But more importantly, she included something else. A recording of her heartbeat, captured as she watched Proxima's sunrise. She wanted you to hear it. She wanted you to know that even at the edge of existence, even as darkness closed in... her heart kept beating."

Maya opened the file. A simple audio track, thirty seconds long. She pressed play.

Thump. Thump. Thump.

A human heart, three thousand years old, beating strong against the silence of space.

"Hermes-7," Maya said when she could speak again. "What happened to the other relays?"

"Relays 1 through 6 reached Earth successfully between 2189 and 2456 CE. Their messages were archived but largely forgotten. Relays 8 through 12 continue toward Earth and will arrive over the next two millennia."

"Why me? Why now?"

"Captain Chen specified that Relay 7 should only activate when it detected a descendant with her genetic markers who had also demonstrated interest in astronomy. Your university application to the Mars Observatory was registered in 2087. The beacon awakened."

Maya laughed through her tears. She'd withdrawn that application after her grandmother's funeral, convinced that practical concerns mattered more than dreams.

"Hermes-7, is there any way to respond?"

"Negative. The communication window is one-way. However..." The AI paused, something almost like hesitation in its synthetic voice. "Captain Chen left a final instruction. She asked that I tell her descendant: 'The stars are not above you. They are ahead of you. Keep walking.'"

Maya sat in the dusty attic, holding a three-thousand-year-old heartbeat in her hand, and understood what her grandmother had been trying to tell her all those years. The old woman had kept that radio dusted and powered. She'd known. Or hoped. Or simply believed that wonder was worth waiting for.

"Thank you, Yuki," Maya whispered. "I'll keep walking."

The radio returned to silence, its mission complete. But in Maya's chest, something ancient and star-born had begun to beat again.

She picked up her phone and redialed the Mars Observatory.
